q: How do you tag a data asset in Unity Catalog's Catalog Explorer, and what does the lineage graph show?
a: In Catalog Explorer you navigate to any table, column, schema, or catalog, open the Overview tab, and click the tag icon to apply key-value tags or system-defined classifications. Tags are stored as metadata on the securable object and can be used to drive dynamic data access policies — for example, column-level masks that fire whenever a column is tagged 'PII'. The lineage graph, accessed via the Lineage tab, shows a directed acyclic graph (DAG) of upstream sources and downstream consumers captured automatically by Unity Catalog at runtime for every SQL, Python, and Spark query run in the workspace. Lineage is captured at both the table level and the column level, so you can trace exactly which source column feeds a derived column in a downstream view. Lineage data captured after September 1, 2024 is retained indefinitely in the metastore and can also be queried programmatically via the system.access.table_lineage and system.access.column_lineage system tables.
fj: Saying tags must be set via SQL ALTER TABLE ... SET TAGS and that lineage only tracks table-level dependencies, not column-level.
fs: Tags can be applied both through Catalog Explorer UI and SQL, but the UI approach is more discoverable for analysts. More importantly, Unity Catalog captures column-level lineage automatically — not just table-level — which is a key governance differentiator.

q: A data governance team wants to mark all columns containing personal data with a 'sensitivity=high' tag so row-level filters auto-apply. Walk through the Catalog Explorer workflow and explain how tags connect to policies.
a: First, navigate to Catalog Explorer, find the target table, and open the Schema tab. For each column, click the edit icon and add the tag 'sensitivity' with value 'high'. Tags are inherited downward in the namespace hierarchy, so tagging at the schema level applies to all tables within it. Next, in the Data Governance section, create a row filter or column mask function that checks CURRENT_USER() or group membership, and bind it to the tagged columns using ALTER TABLE ... ALTER COLUMN ... SET MASK. The dynamic mask fires at query time for every user who lacks elevated privilege. Unity Catalog also supports autoclassification, which uses ML to automatically suggest PII tags for columns that look like emails, SSNs, or phone numbers, reducing manual tagging burden. Auditors can verify coverage by querying system.information_schema.column_tags or the Unity Catalog lineage system tables to confirm all sensitive columns are accounted for.
fj: Saying you need to write a custom Spark job to scan columns and tag them, and that row filters must be hard-coded in view definitions.
fs: Unity Catalog's autoclassification reduces manual effort, and column masks are applied as server-side policies at query time — not in views — so they enforce consistently across every query tool that hits the warehouse.

code.label: Apply a column mask bound to a tag-based policy
lines: ALTER COLUMN email SET MASK mask_pii;

q: What is Delta Sharing and how does it let you share data with external systems that do not run Databricks?
a: Delta Sharing is an open protocol developed by Databricks for secure, real-time data sharing across organizations without copying data or requiring recipients to use Databricks. The protocol is open-source and implemented in the Delta Sharing specification, so any client — Snowflake, Trino, Apache Spark, pandas, Power BI — can query shared data directly over HTTPS. On the provider side, you create a Share object in Unity Catalog, add tables, views, materialized views, or volumes to it, then create a Recipient and distribute credentials (bearer tokens or OIDC federation). Recipients query a read-only endpoint and get zero-copy access to the underlying Parquet/Delta files on cloud storage. Databricks supports two modes: Databricks-to-Databricks (recipient uses Unity Catalog, no token needed) and Databricks-to-Open (recipient uses the open protocol with a token or OIDC). New tokens issued since December 2025 expire after one year maximum. The provider retains full control — revoking a recipient's credentials immediately cuts off access — and all access is governed by Unity Catalog's audit logs.
fj: Describing Delta Sharing as an ETL pipeline that copies Delta tables into the recipient's storage account.
fs: Delta Sharing is zero-copy: no data duplication. Providers share metadata and signed URLs pointing to the original files, and recipients query those files directly. This keeps a single source of truth and avoids storage cost duplication.

code.label: Create a share, add a table, and create a recipient
lines: CREATE SHARE acme_share COMMENT 'External partner share';
ALTER SHARE acme_share ADD TABLE catalog.schema.sales_summary;
-- Grant the recipient access to the share
GRANT SELECT ON SHARE acme_share TO RECIPIENT partner_co;

q: Your company wants to share live sales data with a Snowflake-based partner using Delta Sharing. What protocol mode do you choose, and what governance controls remain with the provider?
a: Because Snowflake is a non-Databricks recipient, you use Databricks-to-Open sharing. The provider generates a bearer-token credential (or configures OIDC federation for token-less auth) and sends it to the Snowflake administrator, who uses the Delta Sharing Snowflake connector to mount the shared tables as external tables. On the provider side, all governance lives in Unity Catalog: you can scope which tables and even which partitions are visible to the recipient by adding partition filters to the share definition, and you can revoke access instantly by deleting the recipient object. Since December 2025, open-sharing tokens have a hard one-year maximum expiry, so periodic rotation is mandatory. All recipient queries appear in the Delta Sharing audit logs under system.access.audit, giving the provider full visibility into what data was accessed and when. Data itself never leaves the provider's cloud storage — the recipient only receives presigned URLs to read specific files.
fj: Saying you should use the Databricks-to-Databricks mode with Snowflake because both are cloud data platforms.
fs: Databricks-to-Databricks mode requires the recipient to have a Unity Catalog-enabled Databricks workspace. Snowflake uses the open protocol (REST + presigned URLs) and is explicitly a Databricks-to-Open recipient.

q: What is Databricks Partner Connect, and what does it offer for data ingestion use cases?
a: Partner Connect is a curated UI inside the Databricks workspace that lets workspace administrators quickly connect validated third-party tools to their Databricks clusters and SQL warehouses with minimal setup. For data ingestion, partners in the 'Data Ingestion' category (such as Fivetran, Airbyte, dbt Cloud, and Rivery) can create databases, tables, service principals, and PAT tokens in the workspace automatically via the Partner Connect provisioning flow. The analyst or admin clicks 'Connect' on a partner tile, approves the required permissions, and Partner Connect provisions a dedicated service principal and passes connection credentials to the partner's SaaS console — no manual cluster configuration or API credential sharing required. Partner Connect does not replace Lakeflow Connect (the native Databricks ingestion connector framework); instead it surfaces ISV-managed pipelines alongside it. It also covers BI tools, data preparation, and ML categories, making it a one-stop integration hub in the workspace sidebar.
fj: Describing Partner Connect as only a marketplace listing page with no automated provisioning.
fs: Partner Connect actively provisions service principals and credentials in your workspace — it is an automated integration wizard, not just a directory of partner links.

q: Compare the five main approaches for bringing data into Databricks: S3/ADLS direct ingestion, Auto Loader, Delta Sharing, Partner Connect / Lakeflow Connect, and Databricks Marketplace.
a: Direct cloud storage ingestion (S3, ADLS, GCS) is the simplest path: you COPY INTO or use Spark batch reads on files already landed in cloud storage, but you must manage file-discovery, deduplication, and schema evolution yourself. Auto Loader solves continuous file ingestion by monitoring cloud storage for new files using either directory listing or SNS/SQS file-notification mode, providing exactly-once semantics, automatic schema inference, and schema evolution — ideal for streaming or micro-batch pipelines with FORMAT_OPTIONS and cloudFiles source. Delta Sharing covers cross-organizational intake where a partner or data provider shares live Delta or Parquet tables directly; the data never copies into your storage, you just mount the share. Lakeflow Connect (formerly called ingestion connectors) is the Databricks-native managed CDC ingestion layer for databases and SaaS APIs, running as serverless pipelines with automatic schema mapping. Databricks Marketplace lets you discover and subscribe to third-party datasets — once a consumer clicks 'Get', the data provider's catalog is shared into your workspace via Delta Sharing with no pipeline to maintain. The right choice depends on latency (Auto Loader for near-real-time), source type (Lakeflow Connect for CDC), or data monetization / discovery (Marketplace).
fj: Saying Auto Loader and Lakeflow Connect are the same product with different names.
fs: Auto Loader is a Structured Streaming source for incrementally ingesting files from cloud storage into Delta. Lakeflow Connect is a managed CDC and SaaS connector framework for database and application sources. They solve different ingestion problems.

code.label: Auto Loader — read new JSON files from S3 with schema inference

q: What is a materialized view in Databricks SQL, how does it differ from a regular view, and what are its refresh options?
a: A materialized view (MV) in Databricks SQL is a precomputed result set stored as a Delta table that can be queried like a table, with results automatically or manually refreshed when upstream data changes. Unlike a regular view — which re-executes its defining query every time it is queried — an MV stores the output, so reads are fast regardless of how expensive the underlying query is. MVs support incremental refresh for a wide set of query shapes including inner joins, left joins, UNION ALL, GROUP BY, and window functions, meaning only changed rows are reprocessed rather than a full recompute. You create an MV with CREATE MATERIALIZED VIEW ... AS SELECT and can attach a refresh schedule using SCHEDULE EVERY syntax or a quartz cron expression. MVs require a Pro or Serverless SQL warehouse (not Classic) or a Lakeflow Declarative Pipelines pipeline. They live as Unity Catalog objects and respect all UC permissions. REFRESH MATERIALIZED VIEW triggers an on-demand refresh. Use CLUSTER BY AUTO on MVs to let Databricks auto-choose liquid clustering keys for optimal query performance.
fj: Saying a materialized view is just a view with caching enabled and that it refreshes automatically on every query.
fs: A materialized view is physically stored data in Delta format, not a cached query plan. Refresh is triggered on a schedule or manually — not automatically on every read — so stale data is possible between refresh cycles.

code.label: Create a materialized view with a daily refresh schedule
lines: CREATE OR REPLACE MATERIALIZED VIEW catalog.schema.daily_revenue
SCHEDULE EVERY 1 DAY AT TIME ZONE 'America/Chicago'

q: What is a streaming table in Databricks SQL, and when would you choose it over a materialized view?
a: A streaming table in Databricks SQL is a Delta table populated incrementally from a streaming source using Structured Streaming semantics. You create it with CREATE OR REFRESH STREAMING TABLE ... AS SELECT ... FROM STREAM <source>, where the STREAM keyword tells Databricks to process the source incrementally — only new records are appended on each refresh. Streaming tables are ideal when: (1) your source is append-only (cloud storage files via Auto Loader, Kafka, or Delta Change Data Feed); (2) you need low-latency continuous updates; or (3) you cannot afford full recomputes because the dataset is too large. Materialized views, by contrast, support aggregations, joins, and deduplication and can handle source deletes and updates via incremental merge logic. If you need COUNT DISTINCT, complex multi-source joins, or SCD-style upserts on the output, choose a materialized view. Both object types are managed through Unity Catalog and can be defined in SQL editors or Lakeflow Declarative Pipelines. Both also support REFRESH statements and schedules.
fj: Saying streaming tables require a Kafka cluster and cannot read from Delta tables or cloud storage.
fs: Streaming tables in Databricks SQL can read from any source that supports streaming semantics — cloud storage (via Auto Loader patterns), Delta tables (via Change Data Feed STREAM), or Kafka. Kafka is one option, not a requirement.

code.label: Create a streaming table that ingests from cloud storage
lines: CREATE OR REFRESH STREAMING TABLE catalog.schema.raw_clickstream

q: How does Databricks Assistant (now Genie Code) help analysts in the SQL editor and notebooks, and what are its key capabilities?
a: Databricks Assistant — rebranded as Genie Code in March 2026 — is a context-aware AI coding assistant embedded in Databricks Notebooks, the SQL editor, the file editor, and AI/BI Dashboards. In the SQL editor, analysts can highlight a failing query and ask 'Fix this error', or describe what they want in plain English and Genie Code generates SQL against the tables visible in the current Unity Catalog context. It reads your schema, column names, and even sample data to produce contextually correct code rather than generic templates. In notebooks, Genie Code is agentic: it can plan and execute multi-step workflows — retrieve a table, run exploratory code, fix errors, and generate a visualization — from a single natural-language prompt. The Quick Fix feature automatically resolves common SQL syntax errors (trailing commas, missing aliases) inline. Genie Code respects workspace governance: it only sees data the calling user has SELECT on, and all AI-assisted queries are logged in the query history. The new SQL editor (GA in 2026) integrates Genie Code with real-time collaboration, multiple statement results, and inline execution history.
fj: Saying Databricks Assistant only works in notebooks and cannot generate SQL queries — it can only explain existing code.
fs: Databricks Assistant / Genie Code is available in both notebooks and the SQL editor, and it actively generates new SQL and Python code from natural language prompts, not just explaining existing code.

q: A dashboard query is running slowly against an external PostgreSQL database registered in Unity Catalog. How would you query it with federated SQL, and what are the performance limits to be aware of?
a: Lakehouse Federation lets you register external databases as connections and foreign catalogs in Unity Catalog, then query them with standard SQL from Databricks without moving data. First, a workspace admin creates a CONNECTION to PostgreSQL using CREATE CONNECTION with the host, port, database name, and credentials stored in a Databricks secret scope. Then you CREATE FOREIGN CATALOG mapped to a specific PostgreSQL database. Once that foreign catalog appears in Unity Catalog, analysts can query tables in it with three-part notation: SELECT * FROM pg_catalog.public.customers. Databricks pushes predicates and projections down to the external system using Photon's query federation engine, so WHERE clauses and column selections reduce data transferred over the network. However, the full analytical engine (joins between a Delta table and a PostgreSQL foreign table) executes in Databricks after pulling necessary rows across. For large fact tables, consider a nightly sync into Delta rather than real-time federation to avoid latency and egress costs. Federation is best for smaller lookup tables, metadata queries, or exploratory cross-system analytics.
fj: Saying you need to write a Spark JDBC DataFrame job in a notebook to query PostgreSQL, and federated SQL is not supported in the SQL editor.
fs: Lakehouse Federation surfaces external tables directly in Unity Catalog so they are queryable from the SQL editor, notebooks, and dashboards with standard SQL — no custom JDBC DataFrame code needed.

code.label: Register a PostgreSQL connection and foreign catalog
lines: -- Create a foreign catalog mirroring the Postgres DB
-- Analyst queries it like any UC table

q: What are the three SQL warehouse types in Databricks, and what are the key capability differences between Serverless, Pro, and Classic?
a: Databricks SQL offers three warehouse types: Serverless, Pro, and Classic. Serverless SQL warehouses are the recommended default — they start in 2-6 seconds (versus ~4 minutes for Pro), support Photon, Predictive IO, and Intelligent Workload Management (IWM), and the compute infrastructure is managed entirely by Databricks, with billing by DBU-per-second. IWM dynamically queues, prioritizes, and right-sizes queries across a shared compute pool, making Serverless ideal for mixed workloads with bursty concurrency. Pro SQL warehouses support Photon and Predictive IO but lack IWM, so concurrency scaling relies solely on auto-scaling cluster nodes; startup is slower. Classic SQL warehouses support only Photon, with no Predictive IO or IWM, and provide entry-level performance. For configuration, all types support T-shirt cluster sizes (X-Small through 4X-Large), auto-stop (idle timeout), auto-scaling (min/max cluster count), and spot instance usage. Serverless warehouses do not have an auto-stop timer in the traditional sense because there is no persistent cluster to terminate. Materialized views and streaming tables can only be refreshed on Pro or Serverless warehouses.
fj: Saying Classic warehouses support Predictive IO and that all three types have the same startup time.
fs: Classic does not support Predictive IO or IWM — only Photon. Serverless starts in seconds while Pro and Classic take minutes. These differences directly affect cost, latency, and concurrency handling.

q: How do you identify poorly performing queries using Query Insights in the Databricks SQL UI?
a: Query Insights is accessible from the SQL Warehouses page — select a warehouse, then click the 'Query Insights' or 'Query History' tab. It surfaces aggregated metrics: mean duration, P90/P99 latency, queued time, spill-to-disk bytes, and rows read per query or per warehouse over a selectable time window. You can sort by 'Slowest queries' or 'Most expensive queries (DBU cost)' to find outliers quickly. For a specific query, clicking through opens the Query Profile — a DAG visualization of the physical plan operators with metrics like time spent, rows processed, memory peak, and bytes spilled at each node. Nodes highlighted in red or orange indicate bottlenecks (e.g., a hash join that spills, a table scan that reads excessive rows). The profile also shows whether result caching was a hit or miss. For warehouse-level analysis, the system.query.history table in Unity Catalog provides the same metrics programmatically, with columns like execution_duration_ms, rows_produced, spill_bytes, and from_result_cache for building custom monitoring dashboards.
fj: Saying you must run EXPLAIN ANALYZE in a notebook cell to find slow queries because the SQL UI provides no performance details.
fs: The Databricks SQL UI's Query Insights and Query Profile give visual, drill-down performance analysis without any SQL commands. EXPLAIN ANALYZE is useful for query plan inspection but is complementary, not a replacement, for warehouse-level monitoring.

code.label: Query system table for top slow queries on a warehouse

q: How do you analyze a Databricks SQL query execution plan, and what do the key nodes in the Query Profile DAG tell you?
a: You access the Query Profile from Query History by clicking a completed or running query. The profile shows a directed acyclic graph (DAG) of physical plan operators with node-level metrics: Time Spent (wall-clock execution time), Memory Peak, Rows Input/Output, and Bytes Read/Spilled. Key operators to understand: Scan (reads Delta or Parquet files — check if partition pruning and predicate pushdown are working by comparing rows_read vs rows_output); HashAggregate (group-by aggregation — spill to disk indicates insufficient memory); SortMergeJoin vs BroadcastHashJoin (broadcast join is fast; sort-merge means one side was too large to broadcast); PhotonPartialAggregation (Photon-native operator); and Exchange/Shuffle (cross-node data movement — excessive shuffles indicate missing CLUSTER BY or poor partition strategy). You can also use EXPLAIN EXTENDED in the SQL editor to see the full logical and physical plan as text. Predictive Query Execution (PQE), available on Serverless warehouses, monitors task metrics in real time and dynamically adjusts execution to avoid skew and spills — visible in the profile as 'PQE adjusted partition count' annotations.
fj: Reading only the final 'Time Spent' metric on the root node and concluding the bottleneck is at the output stage.
fs: The root node aggregates all child times, so it always shows the longest duration. The true bottleneck is the node with the highest individual 'Time Spent' combined with the highest rows or bytes read — usually a scan or shuffle node deep in the DAG.

code.label: EXPLAIN EXTENDED to inspect the physical plan

q: How does result caching work in Databricks SQL, and how do query history records help you determine if caching is benefiting your workload?
a: Databricks SQL has three caching layers: (1) UI cache — per-user, stores the last result in the browser/SQL editor for instant re-display without re-running; (2) Remote result cache — serverless-only, persists query results as workspace system data across warehouse restarts; results are reused if the underlying Delta table version has not changed since the cached result was produced; (3) Disk cache — local SSD on Pro/Classic warehouses, caches Parquet column chunks read from cloud storage to speed repeated scans of the same data. For the remote result cache, the system.query.history table exposes from_result_cache (boolean) and cache_origin_statement_id (the original query's ID) columns. You can write a query against this table to measure your cache hit rate: COUNT(*) WHERE from_result_cache = TRUE divided by total queries. Dashboard queries benefit greatly — if a dashboard widget re-queries within the cache TTL and the Delta table version has not changed, users see instant results at zero warehouse cost. Cache is automatically invalidated when the table version advances (new data is written).
fj: Saying result caching only works for identical repeated queries within the same user session and is cleared when the warehouse restarts.
fs: The remote result cache on Serverless warehouses persists across warehouse restarts and sessions. Any user running the same logical query against an unchanged Delta table version gets the cached result — it is not session-scoped.

code.label: Measure result cache hit rate over the past 7 days
lines: SUM(CASE WHEN from_result_cache THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS cache_hits,
ROUND(100.0 * SUM(CASE WHEN from_result_cache THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) / COUNT(*), 2)

q: How do you schedule automatic dashboard refreshes in Databricks AI/BI Dashboards, and what permission requirements apply to scheduled runs?
a: In an AI/BI Dashboard, the owner or a user with 'Can Edit' permission opens the dashboard, clicks the schedule icon (clock) in the top-right toolbar, and sets a refresh cadence (hourly, daily, weekly, or custom cron). The schedule stores a snapshot of the published dashboard's queries and re-runs them against the associated SQL warehouse at the specified interval. Crucially, scheduled refresh runs execute under the dashboard publisher's credentials by default, meaning the warehouse and data permissions of the publishing user determine what data the refresh can access. As of 2026, publishers can also run scheduled refreshes under a service principal's credentials — useful for dashboards shared with viewers who lack underlying table access, since the service principal's permissions are used at query time. Viewers receive email subscriptions if they opt in. You can also manage scheduled dashboard updates via the Databricks API (/api/2.0/sql/dashboards) for programmatic control. If the publisher loses access to the SQL warehouse or the underlying tables, scheduled refresh will fail with a permission error visible in the dashboard's run history.
fj: Saying scheduled dashboards always run as the viewer's credentials, so every viewer must have SELECT on all underlying tables.
fs: Scheduled refreshes run as the publisher (or an assigned service principal), not the viewer. This is the designed behavior so publishers can share dashboards broadly without granting every viewer raw table access.

q: Your monthly KPI dashboard needs automatic email subscriptions sent to 50 stakeholders every Monday at 8am. Walk through the full setup and what can go wrong.
a: Open the published dashboard and click 'Schedule' in the toolbar. Set the schedule to 'Weekly, Monday, 8:00 AM' in the desired timezone and select the SQL warehouse that will execute queries. Enable 'Email subscribers', paste the 50 email addresses (or group aliases), and save. The system creates a scheduled job under the dashboard publisher's identity. At 8am Monday the platform re-runs all query tiles using the publisher's data credentials and the selected warehouse, renders the dashboard snapshot, and emails a PDF or link to all subscribers. Potential failures: (1) the publisher's PAT or OAuth token expires — fix by reassigning to a service principal; (2) the SQL warehouse is set to 'On-demand' and takes too long to start — fix by switching to Serverless or enabling 'Keep alive' on a Pro warehouse; (3) the publisher loses table permissions after a UC privilege change — fix by granting explicit SELECT to the publisher or service principal; (4) the dashboard has dynamic parameters with no default values — the refresh job will fail because no user input is available. Always set parameter default values before scheduling.
fj: Saying you should create a Databricks Workflow job with a notebook cell that calls spark.sql() to power the dashboard refresh.
fs: Dashboard scheduled refresh is a native AI/BI Dashboard feature — no separate Workflow job or notebook is needed. Using a notebook job would bypass the dashboard rendering and email delivery system.

q: How do SQL query parameters work in Databricks SQL, and how do you connect them to dashboard widgets?
a: In Databricks SQL, named parameters are prefixed with a colon (e.g., :region, :start_date) and can be placed anywhere a literal value would appear in a SQL query. When a query contains a named parameter, the SQL editor automatically renders a parameter widget above the query area, allowing users to input values without editing SQL. Widget types include String (free text), Integer, Decimal, Date, Date Range (creates :start_date.min and :start_date.max), and Dropdown (static list or dynamically populated from another saved query). In an AI/BI Dashboard, you create a dashboard-level parameter by opening the Parameters panel, defining the parameter name and type, and then binding it to one or more query tiles. When a viewer changes the dashboard parameter widget, all bound query tiles re-execute with the new value — enabling interactive filtering without dashboard rebuilds. Named parameters use :name syntax; the legacy {{name}} mustache syntax is deprecated and should not be used in new dashboards. Parameters are scoped: a parameter defined in one dashboard tile is not automatically visible to other tiles unless promoted to a dashboard-level parameter.
fj: Saying parameters are defined using double curly braces {{param}} and that each tile must have its own separate parameter widget.
fs: New Databricks SQL uses colon-prefixed named parameters (:param), not mustache syntax. Dashboard-level parameters can fan out to multiple tiles simultaneously — you do not need per-tile widgets.

code.label: Parameterized query using named parameter syntax

q: What components make up an AI/BI Dashboard in Databricks, and how do you build a multi-tab layout?
a: An AI/BI Dashboard in Databricks is a next-generation dashboarding surface that replaces legacy Databricks SQL dashboards. It is composed of: (1) a canvas-based layout with drag-and-drop widgets — visualizations (bar, line, pie, scatter, map, heatmap), text/markdown blocks, images, and filter controls; (2) query dataset tiles, where each visualization is backed by a named SQL dataset defined within the dashboard; (3) dashboard-level parameters that fan out across multiple visualizations. Multi-tab layouts are created by clicking the '+' icon at the bottom of the canvas to add a new tab (called a 'Page' in the UI) and then building each page independently with its own widgets, though all pages share the same dataset definitions. This allows you to separate executive summary, operational metrics, and trend analysis into distinct pages without maintaining multiple dashboards. In 2026, authors can also create dashboard variables that allow viewers to swap the field shown on a visualization — useful for 'switch metric' patterns. Authors can copy/paste widgets across pages and dashboards.
fj: Saying AI/BI Dashboards only support a single page and that multi-tab layouts require separate dashboard objects linked by navigation tiles.
fs: AI/BI Dashboards natively support multi-page (multi-tab) layouts within a single dashboard object. Pages are added within the same dashboard, sharing datasets and parameters.

q: How do you embed and publish an AI/BI Dashboard for stakeholders who do not have a Databricks workspace login?
a: Databricks AI/BI Dashboards support two sharing modes: (1) workspace sharing — you grant specific users or groups 'Can View' access in the Share dialog, requiring them to log into the Databricks workspace; (2) embedding — you enable embedding in the workspace admin settings, then generate an iframe embed code from the dashboard's Share dialog. The embed code produces a URL that can be dropped into any internal portal, website, or application. Embedding has two sub-modes: basic embedding (publicly accessible, no authentication, suitable for intranet portals behind your own SSO) and secure embedding (the host application injects a short-lived JWT token so the dashboard inherits the token's user identity and data permissions). For broad external distribution, dashboard authors can publish the dashboard using a service principal's credentials, so all viewers see data through the service principal's permissions rather than their own — enabling controlled data exposure to unauthenticated viewers. Embedded dashboards still leverage Delta Sharing governance and UC row-level filters, so sensitive data remains protected.
fj: Saying external users must get a Databricks workspace account to view any dashboard, and embedding is only available for AI/BI Genie Spaces.
fs: Embedding is available for both AI/BI Dashboards and Genie Spaces, and basic embedding allows unauthenticated viewing of dashboards without any Databricks login for stakeholders inside a trusted internal portal.

q: How do you assign permissions for a Genie Space and distribute it to business users who are unfamiliar with SQL?
a: A Genie Space is shared the same way as other Databricks objects: in the Space's three-dot menu, click 'Share' and add users, service principals, or groups with 'Can View' (query only) or 'Can Edit' (modify the Space configuration). 'Can View' is the appropriate permission for business end-users — they can ask natural-language questions and browse answers, but cannot change the underlying datasets, instructions, or SQL examples the Space is built on. Users receive an email invitation and access the Space directly from the Databricks sidebar under AI/BI → Genie; no SQL knowledge is required to use it. For organizations that want Genie chat embedded in an internal tool, the Genie Conversations API (REST) lets applications send questions programmatically and receive structured answers. Workspace admins can restrict who can create Genie Spaces by controlling the 'Allow creating Genie Spaces' entitlement. As of 2026, custom workspace-level instructions can be set by admins so that every Genie conversation follows consistent vocabulary and style guidelines.
fj: Saying all Genie Space users need 'Can Manage' permission to ask questions, and that business users need to understand SQL to interact with the Space.
fs: 'Can View' is sufficient to query a Genie Space interactively. The point of Genie Spaces is precisely to abstract SQL away — business users type questions in natural language and Genie generates the SQL invisibly.

q: Explain how Databricks AI/BI Genie translates a natural-language question into SQL and what data the Space builder provides to improve accuracy.
a: When a user submits a question in a Genie Space, Genie performs a retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) pipeline grounded in the Space's configuration. The Space builder provides: (1) dataset definitions — pointers to Unity Catalog tables or saved SQL queries that form the query scope; (2) instructions — free-text descriptions of business logic, preferred terminology, and column semantics (e.g., 'active_users means users with last_login within 30 days'); (3) SQL examples (benchmarks) — verified question-answer pairs that teach Genie the correct SQL patterns for common questions; the limit is 500 benchmarks per Space. At query time, Genie retrieves the most relevant instructions and examples based on semantic similarity to the question, constructs a prompt with table schemas and context, and uses an LLM to generate SQL. The generated SQL executes against the workspace SQL warehouse, and Genie presents the result with an explanation in plain language. 'Thinking steps' (GA in 2025) show users the SQL logic in readable language so non-technical viewers can verify the answer. Genie respects Unity Catalog permissions — it can only generate SQL over tables the current user has SELECT on, so it will not expose unauthorized data.
fj: Saying Genie sends the user's raw question to an external LLM API that returns finished SQL with no additional context or guardrails.
fs: Genie is a RAG-based system grounded in Unity Catalog schemas, Space instructions, and curated SQL benchmarks. The LLM output is constrained by the Space's defined datasets and UC permissions — it cannot query tables outside the defined scope.

q: When would you choose a Data Vault schema over star schema or OBT in a Databricks SQL data model?
a: Data Vault 2.0 structures a warehouse into three entity types: Hubs (uniquely identify a core business concept — Customer, Product, Order — using a business key and a hash key), Links (model many-to-many relationships between Hubs with their own hash keys and load timestamps), and Satellites (store descriptive attributes and history for Hubs or Links, always insert-only to preserve full audit history). Choose Data Vault when: (1) you have five or more source systems feeding the same conceptual entities — Data Vault's separation of integration (raw vault) from business rules (business vault) absorbs schema changes in one source without impacting others; (2) you need full audit history and bi-temporal tracking out of the box, since Satellites are append-only by design; (3) sources change schema frequently — Satellites are narrow and additive. For simpler scenarios with one or two source systems, stable schemas, and BI-first consumption, a star schema (fact + dimension tables) or One Big Table (OBT, denormalized flat table) is faster to query and easier to maintain. On Databricks, Delta Lake's MERGE and ACID transactions, plus liquid clustering, make Data Vault loading efficient, and the raw vault / information mart pattern maps naturally to the bronze/silver/gold medallion layers.
fj: Saying Data Vault is the default best practice for all Databricks data models because it is more scalable than star schema.
fs: Data Vault's complexity is justified only at scale and with many heterogeneous sources. For most analyst-facing workloads with a single source of truth, a star schema or OBT delivers faster queries and simpler maintenance.

q: What Unity Catalog roles exist for controlling access to a table, and how does privilege inheritance flow through the 3-level namespace?
a: Unity Catalog privileges are granted on securable objects at any level of the three-tier hierarchy: metastore → catalog → schema → table/view/volume/function. The key privileges for table access are: USE CATALOG (required to access any object inside a catalog), USE SCHEMA (required to access any object inside a schema), SELECT (read rows), MODIFY (insert, update, delete, merge), CREATE TABLE/VIEW/SCHEMA (DDL rights), and ALL PRIVILEGES (grants all applicable privileges on the object). Ownership is separate from privilege grants — the owner can perform all operations on the object and grant privileges on it. Ownership roles include catalog owner, schema owner, and table owner; these are set with ALTER CATALOG/SCHEMA/TABLE ... OWNER TO. Privilege inheritance: granting USE CATALOG on a catalog does not automatically grant USE SCHEMA on child schemas — you must grant each level explicitly for non-owners. However, if you are the catalog owner, you inherit effective control of all child schemas and tables. Row-level and column-level security is layered on top via row filters and column masks defined as SQL functions, bound to specific tables.
fj: Saying that granting SELECT on a catalog automatically gives SELECT on all tables inside it, so schema-level grants are unnecessary.
fs: In Unity Catalog, SELECT on a catalog does not cascade SELECT to tables — it only grants SELECT on the catalog object itself. You must also grant USE SCHEMA and SELECT at the schema or table level. Privilege inheritance in UC is additive, not cascading SELECT.

code.label: Grant minimum required privileges for a data reader
lines: -- Grant USE at catalog and schema levels
GRANT USE CATALOG ON CATALOG prod_catalog TO `analyst_group`;
GRANT USE SCHEMA ON SCHEMA prod_catalog.sales TO `analyst_group`;
-- Grant SELECT on a specific table
GRANT SELECT ON TABLE prod_catalog.sales.orders TO `analyst_group`;

q: Your security audit requires that no analyst can SELECT from tables tagged 'confidential' unless they are in the 'data-stewards' group. How do you enforce this in Unity Catalog?
a: First, tag all confidential tables or columns in Catalog Explorer or via SQL (ALTER TABLE ... SET TAGS). Second, define a row filter or column mask SQL function that checks the caller's group membership: use IS_ACCOUNT_GROUP_MEMBER('data-stewards') to allow access, and return NULL or a redacted constant for non-members. Bind the function to the table with ALTER TABLE ... SET ROW FILTER or ALTER TABLE ... ALTER COLUMN ... SET MASK. This approach is declarative and serverside — it fires at query time for every tool (SQL editor, notebooks, BI connectors, REST API) that accesses the table, without any application code change. For blanket revocation, REVOKE SELECT ON TABLE from the principals and only grant to 'data-stewards'. For auditability, system.access.audit logs every GRANT, REVOKE, and SELECT on UC objects. Data lineage in Catalog Explorer shows all downstream users and queries consuming the confidential table, helping identify over-permissioned consumers.
fj: Creating a separate database schema visible only to data-stewards and duplicating the confidential tables there, revoking access to the original.
fs: Data duplication violates the single-source-of-truth principle and creates synchronization risk. Unity Catalog's row filters and column masks enforce access controls on the original table at query time — no copying required.

code.label: Column mask that restricts confidential columns
lines: ALTER COLUMN salary SET MASK mask_confidential;

q: How do you configure access controls for tables, views, and external locations in Unity Catalog, and what is the purpose of storage credentials?
a: Access control in Unity Catalog is managed via SQL GRANT/REVOKE statements or through Catalog Explorer. For tables and views, the privilege chain is: USE CATALOG → USE SCHEMA → SELECT/MODIFY/ALL PRIVILEGES. Views act as an access control layer — a view can be granted SELECT to users who lack SELECT on the underlying base tables, effectively creating a governed, filtered representation of data. External locations define a mapping between a Unity Catalog securable and a cloud storage path (e.g., s3://my-bucket/data/) and are backed by a storage credential (an IAM role or Azure managed identity configured to give Databricks access to that cloud storage path). To grant a user the ability to read from an external location, you GRANT READ FILES ON EXTERNAL LOCATION my_location TO user. WRITE FILES requires READ FILES to also be granted. External tables and volumes are created inside external locations; their underlying files can only be accessed via a warehouse or cluster that can authenticate using the associated storage credential. Workspace administrators create storage credentials and external locations; data engineers create tables and volumes within them; analysts receive SELECT grants on the resulting UC objects without ever seeing cloud storage credentials.
fj: Saying analysts need to be granted access to the storage credential directly to query external tables built on cloud storage.
fs: Analysts never interact with storage credentials directly. The external location and storage credential are admin-managed plumbing; analysts receive SELECT on Unity Catalog table objects, and UC translates that to cloud storage access transparently.

code.label: Grant SELECT on a view backed by a restricted base table
lines: -- Create a view that filters sensitive rows
-- Grant SELECT on view, not on base table
GRANT USE CATALOG ON CATALOG prod_catalog TO `analysts`;
GRANT USE SCHEMA ON SCHEMA prod_catalog.sales TO `analysts`;
GRANT SELECT ON VIEW prod_catalog.sales.public_orders TO `analysts`;

q: What is the Unity Catalog three-level namespace, and why does understanding it matter for security?
a: Unity Catalog organizes all data assets in a three-level hierarchy: catalog → schema → table/view/volume/function. The full object path always follows catalog.schema.object — for example, prod_catalog.sales.orders. Catalogs sit directly under the metastore and are the primary isolation boundary; workspaces share a metastore but can be restricted to specific catalogs. Schemas (databases) group related objects within a catalog. The namespace matters for security because Unity Catalog's permission model is hierarchical but not automatically cascading for SELECT: a principal must hold USE CATALOG on the catalog and USE SCHEMA on the schema before any object-level privilege is effective. Ownership is also namespace-scoped — a catalog owner implicitly controls all children, while table-level ownership does not confer schema ownership. External sharing via Delta Sharing shares at the table level, so recipients receive access to specific tables inside a catalog.schema, not to the entire catalog. Understanding the three-level namespace helps analysts write fully-qualified queries (avoiding ambiguous references when multiple catalogs exist), and helps admins design least-privilege permission structures.
fj: Saying Unity Catalog uses a two-level namespace (schema.table) like traditional Hive metastore, and that catalog is just a synonym for database.
fs: Unity Catalog adds a third level — catalog — above schema. This is a fundamental architectural change from Hive Metastore, which only has schema.table. Catalogs enable multi-workspace governance, data isolation, and Delta Sharing at a granularity that the two-level Hive namespace cannot provide.

code.label: Use fully qualified three-level namespace in a query
lines: -- Set a default catalog and schema to shorten queries
SELECT * FROM orders LIMIT 10; -- resolves to prod_catalog.sales.orders

q: How do you enforce data sharing governance with Delta Sharing to ensure recipients only access data they are authorized to see?
a: Delta Sharing governance is layered on top of Unity Catalog: shares, recipients, and grants are all UC securable objects, so all access is logged in system.access.audit. The provider controls scope at multiple levels: (1) Share definition — you add only specific tables, views, or volumes to a share; you can further restrict with partition filters so a recipient only sees rows matching their geography or tenant ID; (2) Recipient object — each recipient has a unique identity (bearer token or OIDC), and privileges are granted per share (GRANT SELECT ON SHARE share_name TO RECIPIENT recipient_name); (3) Token expiry — since December 2025, open-sharing bearer tokens have a maximum 1-year lifespan, mandating periodic rotation; (4) Revocation — deleting the recipient object or revoking GRANT immediately stops all access; (5) IP allowlisting — you can restrict a recipient's token to specific IP ranges for additional network-level control. The recipient's queries against the shared data still execute within the provider's Delta Sharing endpoint; the provider does not see recipient-side query details, but does see access events in the audit log. For Databricks-to-Databricks sharing, recipients can apply their own UC row filters on top of shared tables without the provider's involvement.
fj: Saying the provider has no visibility into what data recipients access, and governance only happens on the recipient side.
fs: The provider retains full audit visibility via system.access.audit for all Delta Sharing access events. Governance is primarily provider-side: the provider controls share scope, partition filters, token expiry, and revocation.

code.label: Add a partition-filtered table to restrict recipient data scope
lines: -- Share only rows where region = 'EMEA' with the EU partner
GRANT SELECT ON SHARE global_share TO RECIPIENT eu_partner;

q: How do you use Unity Catalog to discover, query, and manage certified datasets across your organization?
a: Unity Catalog serves as the central catalog for all data assets across all workspaces attached to the same metastore. Analysts discover datasets in Catalog Explorer by browsing the three-level namespace or using the search bar to find tables, views, or volumes by name, column name, tag, or free-text keyword. Certified datasets — tables that data owners have marked as authoritative — are tagged (e.g., tag 'certified=true') and appear prominently in search results. Table owners add descriptions at the table and column level in Catalog Explorer, and these descriptions surface in the SQL editor's autocomplete, so analysts know what each column represents before writing a query. To query a certified dataset, analysts use the fully-qualified three-level name: SELECT * FROM prod_catalog.finance.certified_revenue. Managing certified datasets involves: granting SELECT to appropriate groups via GRANT statements, monitoring usage via system.access.audit and system.information_schema.query_history, using column-level lineage to confirm downstream consumers are using the certified table (not a stale copy), and refreshing data quality metrics through ANALYZE TABLE. Data stewards can subscribe to table change notifications via webhooks (Databricks REST API) to alert when schema or quality degrades.
fj: Saying Unity Catalog is a search engine only — you still need a separate data catalog tool like Alation to query and manage datasets.
fs: Unity Catalog is a fully integrated active metadata catalog: it stores schema, lineage, tags, descriptions, permissions, and quality metrics, and certified datasets are queryable directly from it without any external tool.

q: How do you use Query History in Databricks SQL to diagnose a query that ran fast yesterday but is slow today?
a: Open the SQL warehouse's Query History tab (or navigate to SQL → Query History in the sidebar) and filter by the warehouse ID and a date range covering both yesterday and today. Sort by query text or query hash to find the same logical query across both days. Click each run to compare key metrics side-by-side: execution_duration_ms, queued_duration_ms (time waiting in the warehouse queue), bytes_read, rows_produced, and from_result_cache. Common causes of regression: (1) the result cache was a hit yesterday but a miss today because the Delta table was updated overnight — check from_result_cache; (2) the table grew significantly, adding partitions the query must scan — check bytes_read delta; (3) a new conflicting workload caused queue buildup — compare queued_duration_ms; (4) a recent OPTIMIZE or Z-ORDER run changed data layout, altering the query's physical plan. The Query Profile DAG for each run side-by-side reveals which operator changed (e.g., a BroadcastHashJoin degraded to SortMergeJoin because a dimension table crossed the broadcast threshold). The system.query.history system table supports the same analysis programmatically for trend dashboards.
fj: Saying you must rerun the query manually in a notebook and compare elapsed time by eye, because Databricks stores no historical query metrics.
fs: Databricks SQL persists full query metadata in Query History and the system.query.history table, including per-query operator-level profiles. You never need to re-execute to diagnose a regression.

code.label: Compare two runs of the same query over time
lines: SUM(CASE WHEN from_result_cache THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS cache_hits,

q: What is Databricks Marketplace, and how does it differ from Partner Connect for a data analyst trying to access external datasets?
a: Databricks Marketplace is an open exchange where data providers, software vendors, and research institutions publish listings — datasets, ML models, notebooks, Solution Accelerators, apps, and MCP servers — that workspace consumers can discover, evaluate (with free samples), and subscribe to directly from within their workspace. Once a consumer requests access and the provider approves, the dataset is shared into the consumer's workspace as a Unity Catalog catalog via Delta Sharing — no ETL pipeline, no data copy, zero storage cost. It works for both commercial data products (e.g., weather data, financial indices) and free open datasets. Partner Connect, by contrast, connects a Databricks workspace to third-party SaaS tools — ingestion, BI, transformation — by automatically provisioning service principals and credentials. Partner Connect is about tools and pipelines; Marketplace is about data products and content. A data analyst looking for an external dataset (e.g., Census demographic data) would use Marketplace; an analyst looking to connect Tableau or Fivetran would use Partner Connect.
fj: Saying Marketplace requires you to write a Spark job to copy the dataset into your own Delta tables before you can query it.
fs: Marketplace uses Delta Sharing for zero-copy delivery — the dataset catalog appears directly in your Unity Catalog namespace and is queryable immediately with no ingestion pipeline required.

q: A vendor offers demographic data on Databricks Marketplace. Walk through the end-to-end steps to access it and join it with an internal sales table.
a: First, navigate to Data → Marketplace in the Databricks sidebar or go to marketplace.databricks.com. Search for the demographic dataset, review the listing's sample data and schema documentation, and click 'Get instant access' (free datasets) or 'Request access' (commercial). After the provider approves, Databricks provisions a shared Unity Catalog catalog in your workspace — typically named something like provider_co_demographics — via Delta Sharing with no data copied to your storage. The catalog appears immediately in Catalog Explorer and the SQL editor's autocomplete. You then query it with a standard three-part name: SELECT * FROM provider_co_demographics.us.zip_codes. To join with internal data: SELECT s.region, s.revenue, d.median_income FROM prod_catalog.sales.orders s JOIN provider_co_demographics.us.zip_codes d ON s.zip = d.zip_code. Access is controlled by the provider — if they revoke your entitlement, the catalog disappears from your namespace. Refresh cadence depends on the provider's publishing schedule, visible in the listing metadata. You cannot write to or modify the shared catalog; it is read-only.
fj: Saying you need to export the Marketplace dataset to S3 first and then run COPY INTO to load it before querying.
fs: Marketplace delivery is zero-copy via Delta Sharing. The provider's catalog is mounted directly in your workspace; no download, export, or COPY INTO step is needed. The data is live and queryable immediately.

code.label: Join a Marketplace dataset with an internal Delta table
